title: Song of Solomon
author: Solomon (title, 1:1), though this may indicate Solomonic patronage
setting: A collection of love poems celebrating the beauty of human romantic love between a man and a woman, set in the landscape of Israel.
purpose: To celebrate the goodness of God-given sexual love within marriage as a creation gift, and — in the allegorical reading recognized by both Jewish and Christian tradition — to depict God's covenant love for his people.
themes: The beauty and goodness of human love
The beloved as garden and the lover's delight
God's love for Israel / Christ's love for the church (allegorical)

outline.label: Night and Day: Seeking and Finding
Praise of the Beloved; Love's Triumph

key_people.name: The Beloved (Shulamite)
key_people.role: The young woman whose voice dominates the poem; she pursues her lover with unashamed desire and is presented throughout as the poem's most active and theologically articulate figure

key_people.name: Solomon/The Lover
key_people.role: The young man whose praise of his beloved and expression of mutual desire drives the poem's movement; the Solomonic attribution may be literary and ideal rather than strictly biographical

key_people.name: The Daughters of Jerusalem
key_people.role: A choral presence who draw out the beloved's speech, serving a structural function similar to a Greek chorus — pressing her to articulate what love is

context: Song of Solomon is the OT's only extended poem celebrating erotic love between a man and a woman. Its inclusion in the canon was debated in rabbinic tradition but affirmed by Rabbi Akiva who called it 'the holy of holies of Scripture.' The book was likely composed in the Solomonic era or later as a literary celebration of the love poetry tradition of the ancient Near East. It must be read against Genesis 1–2 (marriage as God's gift) and Genesis 3 (the distortion of that gift), as a vision of what redeemed sexuality looks like.
christ_connection: The allegorical reading of Song of Solomon — God as Lover, Israel/the Church as beloved — has deep roots in both Jewish (Akiva, the Exodus and Sinai as God's courtship) and Christian (Origen, Bernard of Clairvaux, Jonathan Edwards) interpretation and is not merely imposed on the text but invited by the prophets who consistently use marriage as the primary metaphor for the covenant (Hosea, Ezekiel, Isaiah 54, 62; Ephesians 5; Revelation 19–22). Christ is the Bridegroom who pursues his people with costly love; the Church is the bride being prepared for the wedding supper of the Lamb.

key_verses.note: 'My beloved is mine, and I am his' — the covenant formula of mutual belonging that captures the book's central movement from separation to union
'Love is strong as death... Many waters cannot quench love' — the theological climax: love is the most powerful force in human experience, and the only one that can overcome death's dominance

themes_detail.title: The Goodness of Embodied, Erotic Love
themes_detail.text: Song of Solomon is an unapologetic celebration of physical desire between a man and a woman — their bodies, their scent, their voices, their longing for each other. This is not incidental to the canon but essential to it: the book stands as a permanent rebuttal to any theology that spiritualizes away bodily reality or treats sexuality as inherently fallen. The love described here is unashamed, and the poem's repeated refrain ('his left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me') is erotic without being pornographic — it portrays desire as proper and beautiful within its context.

themes_detail.title: The Covenant Dynamic of Pursuit and Belonging
themes_detail.text: The poem's movement is not a linear narrative but a series of longing, seeking, finding, and belonging — with recurring motifs of the absent lover, the nighttime search, and the joy of reunion. This dynamic of desire, separation, and consummation mirrors the covenant relationship between God and his people throughout Scripture: God pursues; his people wander; reunion is costly but glorious. The refrain 'Do not awaken love until it pleases' (2:7; 3:5; 8:4) marks love as something that operates on its own proper timing, not human impatience.

themes_detail.title: Love's Power and Vulnerability
themes_detail.text: The book's final verses (8:6–7) rise to a theological declaration: love is as strong as death, its jealousy as fierce as the grave. Love cannot be quenched by many waters, cannot be bought with wealth. This is not merely romantic poetry but a theological claim about the nature of covenantal love — it is the most powerful, most costly, and most durable force in human experience. Set in the context of the whole canon, it points forward to the love of Christ, which is stronger than death precisely because it overcame it.

themes_detail.title: Beauty, Creation, and the Praise of the Other
themes_detail.text: The extended physical descriptions of the beloved and the lover (chs. 4, 5, 7) are acts of attentive, generous praise that see the other as beautiful. This practice of beholding and articulating the beauty of one's beloved is itself a form of worship — it reflects back to God his own delight in what he has made. The countryside and its creatures (vineyards, gazelles, foxes, flowers, mountains) are not merely backdrop but woven into the texture of desire, suggesting that erotic love properly experienced is continuous with wonder at creation.
